hook: You already know how to find the value of a function at a point — just plug in the number. But what happens when you *can't* plug in, like when the expression becomes $\frac{0}{0}$? This unit gives you the powerful tool mathematicians use to reason about where a function is *headed*, even when it never quite arrives.
concept: Every topic in this unit revolves around one central idea: describing the behavior of a function as its input gets arbitrarily close to some value, captured formally by the concept of a *limit*, $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L$.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to describe what value a function approaches as $x$ gets close to a given point, using tables and graphs to build genuine intuition before any formulas appear.
Master techniques like factoring, rationalizing, and canceling common factors to evaluate limits exactly — including the tricky $\frac{0}{0}$ cases where direct substitution fails.

You will learn to analyze limits from the left and right separately, and use the agreement of those one-sided limits to give a precise meaning to what it means for a function to be continuous at a point.

Apply limit techniques to describe functions that grow without bound or settle toward a horizontal asymptote, connecting the symbolic notation $\lim_{x \to \infty}$ to the long-run behavior you can see on a graph.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Epsilon-Delta Definition of a Limit (H)
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to write rigorous $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ proofs that make the intuitive idea of 'approaching a value' mathematically airtight — the foundation on which all of calculus is formally built.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Continuity and the Intermediate Value Theorem
topic_roadmap.description: Apply the formal definition of continuity to classify different types of discontinuities, and use the Intermediate Value Theorem to prove that solutions to equations must exist within an interval.
